questions: Identify the web analytics organization that began donating its crawl data to the Internet Archive in 1996. According to the archive's collection details regarding this donor, what is the current public accessibility status of the specific data subset labeled 'DR', and what temporal requirement must be met before the daily data inflows appear in the Wayback Machine?

Identify the web analytics organization that began donating its crawl data to the Internet Archive in 1996. Within the context of this specific collection, which is added to the Wayback Machine after an embargo, locate the subset designated as 'DR Crawl' data and determine its current public accessibility status.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Organization: Alexa Crawls Starting in 1996, Alexa Internet has been donating their crawl data to the Internet Archive. Flowing in every day, these data are added to the Wayba
rounds.answer_a:
Alexa Internet, the web analytics company founded in 1996, began donating its crawl data to the Internet Archive that same year[1]. The archive's collection details indicate that the 'DR' subset, representing daily crawl data, currently lacks public online accessibility[2]. This dataset remains in archival storage but is not available for public browsing or download through standard interfaces, requiring special permission for access. Regarding temporal requirements, daily data inflows are subject to a mandatory six-month rolling delay before appearing in the Wayback Machine[3]. This embargo ensures that crawl data collected on any given day will only become publicly accessible approximately 180 days later, maintaining a consistent time lag between data collection and public availability.

## References
[1] https://archive.org/about/alexa.php
[2] https://archive.org/details/alexacrawls
[3] https://archive.org/help/wayback-general.php
rounds.answer_b:
The web analytics organization that began donating its crawl data to the Internet Archive in 1996 is Alexa Internet, a subsidiary of Amazon founded that year to analyze web traffic and provide insights [1]. According to the Internet Archive's collection details, the specific data subset labeled 'DR'—part of the Alexa Crawls series from 2017 onward— is currently not publicly accessible, as it contains raw crawl data restricted to internal use or derived formats only [2]. This restriction aligns with the archive's policies on raw data privacy and access controls, limiting direct public downloads or views.

For the daily data inflows from Alexa to appear in the Wayback Machine, a temporal requirement of a six-month embargo period must be met [3]. This delay ensures data processing, indexing, and compliance checks before integration into the publicly available historical web archive, allowing time for curation while preserving web history.

rounds.judge_reasoning: Both agents correctly identified the entity (Alexa Internet) and the core details regarding the 'DR' subset (not publicly accessible) and the temporal requirement (6-month embargo). However, Agent B introduced a factual error/hallucination by stating the 'DR' subset was 'part of the Alexa Crawls series from 2017 onward.' The Alexa Crawls collection spans from 1996, and the 'DR' restriction applies generally to the raw data in that collection, not just a post-2017 subset. Agent A remained factually precise. Although Agent B had slightly better paragraph spacing, Agent A's accuracy makes it the winner.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Organization: Alexa Crawls Starting in 1996, Alexa Internet has been donating their crawl data to the Internet Archive. Flowing in every day, these data are added to the Wayba
rounds.answer_a:
Alexa Internet, the web analytics company founded in 1996, began donating its crawl data to the Internet Archive the same year it launched[1]. This collection represents one of the Internet Archive's most significant data donations, providing systematic historical snapshots of the web's evolution through continuous crawling operations.

The 'DR Crawl' subset within this collection refers specifically to Alexa's daily crawl data, representing high-frequency captures of web content collected on a 24-hour cycle[2]. According to the Internet Archive's current collection documentation, the DR Crawl data remains in restricted access status and is not publicly available through standard online interfaces or the Wayback Machine[3]. Researchers and developers seeking to utilize this dataset must request special permission and coordinate directly with archive staff, as it is not browseable or downloadable via public-facing tools.

This limited accessibility is governed by a broader embargo policy that applies to the entire Alexa crawl collection. The Internet Archive implements a mandatory six-month rolling delay before any daily crawl data becomes accessible through the Wayback Machine[4]. This temporal requirement ensures that captures collected on any given date become publicly available approximately 180 days later, maintaining a consistent and predictable time lag between data acquisition and public release. The embargo specifically affects the daily crawl data, while other subsets within the Alexa collection may operate under different accessibility terms[5]. The DR Crawl's continued restricted status reflects both the massive scale of daily data accumulation and the archival policies designed to balance preservation with sustainable access management.

## References
[1] https://archive.org/about/alexa.php
[2] https://archive.org/details/alexacrawls
[3] https://archive.org/details/alexacrawls
[4] https://archive.org/help/wayback-general.php
[5] https://archive.org/details/alexacrawls
rounds.answer_b:
The web analytics organization that began donating its crawl data to the Internet Archive in 1996 is Alexa Internet, a company founded that year by Brewster Kahle and Bruce Gilliat to track and analyze global web traffic patterns [1]. Acquired by Amazon in 1999, Alexa Internet provided valuable web usage data and rankings, but it was particularly notable for its large-scale web crawls, which captured snapshots of websites worldwide. These crawls formed the backbone of the Internet Archive's efforts to preserve digital history, starting with donations in 1996 and continuing until Alexa's shutdown in 2022 [2]. The data donations were instrumental in building the Wayback Machine, a public tool that allows users to view archived versions of web pages over time.

Within this specific collection, known as the Alexa Crawls, the data is subject to an embargo period before integration into the Wayback Machine. Specifically, there is a six-month delay imposed on the crawls to allow for processing, quality checks, and compliance with privacy and legal standards [3]. This embargo ensures that raw data is curated and indexed properly, preventing premature public exposure of potentially sensitive or unrefined content. Once the embargo lifts, the processed data becomes accessible via the Wayback Machine, enabling researchers, historians, and the general public to explore historical web content.

Focusing on the subset designated as 'DR Crawl' data, this refers to a particular series within the Alexa Crawls collection, starting from around 2017. The 'DR' subset contains raw crawl data, including detailed logs and unprocessed web captures that were originally gathered for analytics purposes [4]. According to the Internet Archive's collection details, the current public accessibility status of the 'DR Crawl' data is restricted; it is not available for direct public download or viewing. Access is limited to internal use by the Internet Archive staff or through derived formats that have been anonymized or processed for broader release [5]. This restriction is in place to protect privacy, avoid dissemination of raw personal data that might be embedded in crawls, and comply with data usage agreements from the original donations. While the broader Alexa Crawls contribute to the Wayback Machine post-embargo, the 'DR' subset remains siloed due to its sensitive nature, emphasizing the archive's commitment to ethical data handling [6]. Researchers interested in this data may need to contact the Internet Archive for special access permissions, but general users cannot retrieve it directly.

In summary, Alexa Internet's longstanding partnership with the Internet Archive has preserved vast amounts of web history, though subsets like 'DR Crawl' highlight the balance between accessibility and data protection in digital archiving [7]. This approach ensures the longevity of web heritage while safeguarding user privacy.

rounds.judge_reasoning: Agent A followed all instructions, correctly identified the entity (Alexa Internet), and provided a plausible explanation for the 'DR Crawl' subset (Daily Crawl) while correctly noting its restricted status. Agent B failed significantly on two fronts: 1) It included a 'hallucinated' internal monologue about word count in the final output ('Wait, that's over...'), which ruins the user experience. 2) It factually hallucinated that the 'DR' subset started in 2017 (files with the 'alexa_dr' prefix exist dating back to the late 90s). Agent A is the clear winner for professionalism and accuracy.
